question_text: Explain why the current is different through each resistor when resistors of different values are connected in parallel, even though the voltage across each is the same.
correct_answer: Because current depends on both voltage and resistance (I = V/R). In parallel, voltage is constant, but resistors with lower resistance allow more current to flow through them. A lower resistance provides an easier path for electrons.
explanation: This tests understanding of Ohm's law in the context of parallel circuits. Students must recognise that resistance determines how much current flows for a given voltage—lower resistance means higher current for the same voltage.
